title: Tall Panel Headboard (Queen)
summary: A wall-anchored, floor-to-ceiling-scale queen headboard with a plywood center panel framed in moulding.
description:
This headboard is genuinely tall, which is what gives it its presence — and also why wall-anchoring the top isn't optional. The plan calls it out twice for a reason: an unanchored tall headboard is a forward-tipping hazard the moment weight leans against it, so the top rail gets lag-bolted into wall studs before it's considered finished, not as an afterthought.

The center panel is plywood, not a solid glued-up slab, which keeps the piece from warping at this height and width — solid wood this large would move with humidity in ways that crack a frame. Drill the leg pocket holes facing upward before you attach anything else; trying to add them after the frame is assembled means reaching into a mostly-closed box. The baseboard moulding used as trim needs extra length beyond the finished measurement to account for miter waste — buy more than the cut list states.

tools.note: For mitering the baseboard and header moulding corners.

materials.name: Pine or fir, 2x4, 8 ft (or stud length)
Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Center panel — full sheet keeps this piece from warping the way a solid glued-up slab would at this height.

materials.name: Baseboard moulding, 5-1/4"
materials.note: Buy extra beyond the finished measurement — mitered corners waste real length.

materials.name: Lag screws
materials.note: Anchors the top rail to wall studs — not optional at this height.

cutList.note: Measure and cut to fit — 1x4 width varies board to board.

steps.title: Attach the legs to the top 2x4
steps.body: IMPORTANT: because of this headboard's height, the top must be secured to wall studs to prevent forward tipping — plan the wall anchor point now, before you build. Attach the legs to the top 2x4, and while you're at it, drill 1-1/2" pocket holes facing upward for attaching the top cap in a later step — much easier to drill now than once the frame is assembled.
materials: Pine or fir, 2x4, 8 ft (or stud length)

steps.title: Attach the front trim
steps.body: Attach the two 1x4 front trim pieces, keeping everything flush to the front face.

steps.title: Fit and attach the center trim
steps.body: Measure and cut the center 1x4 trim piece to fit — 1x4 stock varies in actual width board to board, so cutting to your own measurement beats trusting the plan's number.

steps.title: Cut and attach the back panel
steps.body: Cut the plywood center panel to fit and attach it to the legs and header. Also attach it to the front 1x4 trim boards with 1-1/4" screws — pocket hole screws work fine here even though the joint isn't a pocket hole, saving a trip for a different screw type.

steps.title: Add the panel moulding
steps.body: Attach moulding inside the 1x4 frames, mitering the corners for a clean finish.

steps.title: Attach the header moulding and anchor to the wall
steps.body: Attach crown or header moulding to the top of the headboard. Finally — and this bears repeating — secure the top to wall studs with the lag screws before this headboard goes into use. Its height makes an unanchored top a real tipping hazard.

images.alt: Tall queen bed headboard with a plywood center panel framed in moulding
